title: About this site
heading: (intro)
text:
import DensityStatus from '~/components/DensityStatus.astro'; import HeartbeatStatus from '~/components/HeartbeatStatus.astro'; import { MAINTAINER_NAME, REPO_URL } from '~/config/site'; This site explains AI governance in plain language and tracks the world's authoritative regulations and vendor frameworks. It is maintained by one person — {MAINTAINER_NAME} — as an open, no-tracking, no-ads, public knowledge resource.

Sustainability

**This site is maintained by one person.** If something breaks, expect 24–72 hours to a fix during normal operations. During declared sustainability mode (banner visible site-wide), expect a weekly cadence instead of daily. If maintenance pauses for longer than two weeks, the README will say so. Co-maintainer wanted → See MAINTENANCE.md for the full operational model: weekly review cap, escalation path, graceful sunset plan.

Density

**Public launch is gated on density.** The site stays private until: 12 stage explainers authored • ≥60 glossary terms • ≥30 regulation snapshots tracked.

Operations

The pipeline runs unattended on a schedule and surfaces noise-free PRs for review. Per-source heartbeats track when each scheduled job last ran successfully.

title: About this site
heading: How to contribute
text: See CONTRIBUTING.md. Briefly: edit the Markdown, follow the voice rules in docs/STYLE.md (no prescriptive personal address, no marketing words), open a PR. The site has zero tracking and never will. No cookies, no analytics that retain PII, no third-party CDN.

title: About this site
heading: Disclaimer
text: This site explains AI governance. It is not legal advice. Consult qualified counsel before acting on any content.

title: 1. AI Policy
heading: (intro)

title: 1. AI Policy
heading: What the policy actually contains
text:
An AI policy is not a marketing statement. It is the binding internal document that names:

- The categories of AI systems your organisation will build, buy, or deploy.
- The risk tiers (see [Risk Tiering](/stages/02-risk-tiering/)) that gate approval.
- The owners of each downstream control: red-teaming, monitoring, incident response.
- What is explicitly off-limits (e.g., emotion recognition in workplaces, biometric categorisation).

<PersonaSection persona="exec">
The policy is the artefact your board, your insurer, and your auditor will all read first. It is more durable than any quarterly KPI. Treat it as a controlled document with version history.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="engineer">
Every system you build will be classified against this policy. The earlier engineering input lands in the policy text, the less rework later. Push back on language that conflates "high-risk" tier with "anything using ML."
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="compliance">
The AI policy is the parent document for your AIMS (per ISO/IEC 42001). It satisfies the EU AI Act's Article-9 risk-management-system documentation requirements when paired with the per-system risk file.
</PersonaSection>

title: 1. AI Policy
heading: The fictional company perspective
text:
[Sigma Health Berlin](/stories/sigma-health/) drafted their AI policy in eight weeks. The hardest section was the off-limits list — leadership wanted to "leave options open" while regulatory counsel pushed for explicit exclusions. They landed on a hybrid: a narrow off-limits list plus a "needs board approval" gate for novel use-cases.

<RegQuote source="iso-iec-42001" article="Clause-5.2" snapshot="2026-05-24" regulationName="ISO/IEC 42001">
Top management shall establish, implement and maintain an AI policy that is appropriate to the purpose of the organisation, provides a framework for setting AI objectives, includes a commitment to satisfy applicable requirements, and includes a commitment to continual improvement of the AI management system.
</RegQuote>

**Why this matters:** the policy is the only thing that holds when the team that built the system leaves. Make it short, make it readable, and make sure the owners named in it actually know they're named.

---

This stage is the entry point. The next stage — [Risk Tiering](/stages/02-risk-tiering/) — is where the policy starts having teeth.

title: 2. Risk Tiering
heading: (intro)

title: 2. Risk Tiering
heading: The four tiers
text:
The EU AI Act uses four tiers (unacceptable, high-risk, limited-risk, minimal-risk). Other frameworks differ — NIST AI RMF talks about "context" rather than discrete tiers; ISO/IEC 42001 leaves the tiering taxonomy to your AIMS. The discipline is the same: classify once, write down the reasoning, revisit when context changes.

<PersonaSection persona="exec">
The tier determines whether a system can ship in days or months. Push for clarity on this decision early — every week the classification stays ambiguous costs engineering velocity and counsel time.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="engineer">
Tiering is not based on model size or training-data volume. It is based on use-context: who the system affects, what decisions it influences, what the downside of error looks like. A 1B-parameter model deciding loan eligibility is high-risk; a 70B-parameter model summarising internal meeting notes is not.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="compliance">
EU AI Act Article 6 plus Annex III are the operative texts. Annex III lists eight high-risk use-case categories. Crucially, Article 6(3) lets you derogate from high-risk classification with a written and registered justification — that's a paper exercise you cannot afford to skip when you believe it applies.
</PersonaSection>

title: 2. Risk Tiering
heading: Fictional company
text:
[Aurora Insurance London](/stories/aurora-insurance/) tiers their claim-fraud detection model as high-risk under Annex III §5(b). Their LLM-based customer-service summariser is limited-risk (transparency obligations only). Both decisions are documented in a one-page tier rationale stored next to the model card.

<RegQuote source="eu-ai-act" article="Art-6" snapshot="2026-05-24" regulationName="EU AI Act">
An AI system shall be considered to be high-risk where both of the following conditions are fulfilled: (a) the AI system is intended to be used as a safety component of a product, or is itself a product, covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I; (b) the product whose safety component is the AI system, or the AI system itself as a product, is required to undergo a third-party conformity assessment.
</RegQuote>

**Why this matters:** misclassification is the single most expensive mistake in this entire journey — every downstream control depends on the tier. Get a second pair of eyes on Annex III mapping before you commit.

---

Next: [Risk Check](/stages/03-risk-check/) — the periodic re-evaluation of the tier you just assigned.

title: 3. Risk Check
heading: (intro)

title: 3. Risk Check
heading: When risk check fires
text:
- After any change to model, training data, or system prompt that exceeds your declared change-control threshold.
- On a calendar cadence aligned with the tier — typically quarterly for high-risk, annually for limited-risk.
- When a downstream incident report (Stage 11) finds the tier was wrong.

<PersonaSection persona="exec">
Risk check is the cheapest moment in the lifecycle to catch a tier mistake. Once a misclassified system is in production, the legal and operational cost of re-tiering compounds. Budget the recurring time.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="engineer">
Tie the risk check to your release pipeline. A model retrain that crosses your declared drift threshold is the most common trigger. Wire a flag into your training infra that pings the risk owner when drift exceeds the threshold.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="compliance">
EU AI Act Article 9(2) requires the risk-management system to be a continuous iterative process throughout the life cycle. That language reads as "annual" but bites as "any material change" — your auditor will look at the change log first.
</PersonaSection>

title: 3. Risk Check
heading: Fictional company
text:
[Acme Robotics US](/stories/acme-robotics/) ran their first quarterly risk check on the warehouse-navigation model six months after launch. The check caught that the model had been silently retrained on data from a new facility that included near-miss-with-human events. The tier shifted from limited to high-risk; the conformity assessment took six weeks; deployment paused in the meantime.

<RegQuote source="eu-ai-act" article="Art-9" snapshot="2026-05-24" regulationName="EU AI Act">
A risk management system shall be established, implemented, documented and maintained in relation to high-risk AI systems. The risk management system shall be understood as a continuous iterative process planned and run throughout the entire lifecycle of a high-risk AI system, requiring regular systematic review and updating.
</RegQuote>

**Why this matters:** every misclassified system you discover after launch costs more to fix than every classification you got right. Make the recurring check non-negotiable.

---

Next: [Compliance](/stages/04-compliance/) — building the controls that the tier just demanded.

title: 4. Compliance
heading: (intro)

title: 4. Compliance
heading: What compliance ships
text:
For high-risk EU AI Act systems, the conformity-assessment file is the deliverable. It contains:

- A data-governance record (Art. 10) — lineage, bias evaluation, quality criteria
- Technical documentation (Art. 11 + Annex IV) — design, architecture, validation results
- Transparency information for deployers (Art. 13)
- Human-oversight procedures (Art. 14)

For voluntary frameworks like NIST AI RMF, the equivalent artefact is the AI risk profile organised across Govern / Map / Measure / Manage.

<PersonaSection persona="exec">
Compliance is the line item that scales linearly with the number of high-risk systems you operate. Budget per-system, not per-organisation.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="engineer">
Treat the technical documentation as a living artefact, generated where possible from model cards and pipeline metadata. Hand-written documentation rots within one release cycle.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="compliance">
Article 11 + Annex IV is the canonical structure for high-risk technical documentation. Map your existing model-card schema to its sections before authoring fresh prose.
</PersonaSection>

title: 4. Compliance
heading: Fictional company
text:
[Sigma Health Berlin](/stories/sigma-health/) maintains their conformity assessment as a git-tracked Markdown bundle. Each release tag produces a frozen PDF; the canonical source is reviewed monthly by their notified body.

<RegQuote source="eu-ai-act" article="Art-11" snapshot="2026-05-24" regulationName="EU AI Act">
The technical documentation of a high-risk AI system shall be drawn up before that system is placed on the market or put into service and shall be kept up-to-date.
</RegQuote>

**Why this matters:** drafting compliance evidence after a regulator asks for it is an order of magnitude more expensive than maintaining it as a build artefact.

---

Next: [Third-party AI Risk](/stages/05-third-party-risk/) — applying the same lens to vendors.

title: 5. Third-party AI Risk
heading: (intro)

title: 5. Third-party AI Risk
heading: Vendor governance checklist
text:
For every AI vendor:

- What contractual data-use limits are in place? Are training-on-your-data rights explicitly excluded?
- What is the vendor's own AI policy? (Many will share on request.)
- Where is the model hosted, and which jurisdiction's law applies?
- How do you receive notice of material model changes?

<PersonaSection persona="exec">
The procurement contract is where this work happens. Bake AI-specific clauses into your standard MSA before the next vendor renewal — retroactive amendments rarely succeed.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="engineer">
Build a vendor inventory at the system level, not the contract level. One vendor selling three products with three different model architectures requires three risk records.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="compliance">
EU AI Act Article 25(1) shifts you from deployer to provider when you substantially modify a high-risk system. Fine-tuning a third-party foundation model often crosses that line. Document the boundary explicitly.
</PersonaSection>

title: 5. Third-party AI Risk
heading: Fictional company
text:
[Densha Logistics Tokyo](/stories/densha-logistics/) uses a third-party route-optimisation model from a Singapore vendor. Their vendor contract requires 30 days' notice of any change to the model's training data or architecture. The notice triggers an internal risk-tier re-check (Stage 3).

<RegQuote source="eu-ai-act" article="Art-25" snapshot="2026-05-24" regulationName="EU AI Act">
Any distributor, importer, deployer or other third-party shall be considered to be a provider of a high-risk AI system for the purposes of this Regulation and shall be subject to the obligations of the provider under Article 16, in any of the following circumstances: (a) they put their name or trademark on a high-risk AI system already placed on the market or put into service; (b) they make a substantial modification to a high-risk AI system that has already been placed on the market or has already been put into service.
</RegQuote>

**Why this matters:** the provider/deployer line determines who carries the conformity-assessment burden. Cross it accidentally and you inherit obligations you did not budget for.

---

Next: [Data Controls](/stages/06-data-controls/) — the substrate every AI system runs on.

title: 6. Data Controls
heading: (intro)

title: 6. Data Controls
heading: What good data control looks like
text:
- **Lineage**: every training dataset traceable to its source, including consent or licence basis.
- **Bias evaluation**: documented baseline performance across protected attributes.
- **Lawful basis**: under UK GDPR / EU GDPR, an explicit lawful basis recorded per data category.
- **Retention**: timed deletion + suppression-list integration with model retraining.

<PersonaSection persona="exec">
Data controls are the most likely place for a regulator to look first. They are also the cheapest to fix early and the most expensive to retrofit. Treat lineage as a first-class engineering deliverable.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="engineer">
Lineage starts at the ingestion pipeline, not at the model trainer. Tag every dataset with source URI, fetch timestamp, licence string, and the consent transaction ID where applicable.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="compliance">
UK GDPR's DPIA threshold is essentially always triggered for AI processing. The ICO's AI guidance suite walks through lawful-basis selection, accuracy testing, and ADM transparency in operational detail.
</PersonaSection>

title: 6. Data Controls
heading: Fictional company
text:
[Aurora Insurance London](/stories/aurora-insurance/) operates a single Data Controls Register keyed by dataset name. The register links to lineage, DPIA, bias-evaluation report, and the consuming model cards. Removing or modifying a dataset triggers a downstream re-check across every model that consumes it.

<RegQuote source="eu-ai-act" article="Art-10" snapshot="2026-05-24" regulationName="EU AI Act">
High-risk AI systems which make use of techniques involving the training of models with data shall be developed on the basis of training, validation and testing data sets that meet the quality criteria referred to in paragraphs 2 to 5 whenever such data sets are used.
</RegQuote>

**Why this matters:** the data is the regulation's centre of gravity. If you cannot prove what data trained the model, you cannot defend the model's behaviour.

---

Next: [Continuous Red-teaming](/stages/07-red-teaming/) — adversarial probing of the system you just built.

title: 7. Continuous Red-teaming
heading: (intro)

title: 7. Continuous Red-teaming
heading: Adversarial-ML control catalogue (TRK-17)
text:
This site cross-lists four open catalogues. Each row maps a category of failure to representative entries across all four — pick one as the canonical source-of-truth for your programme; the others are sanity references.

| Failure category | OWASP LLM Top 10 | MITRE ATLAS | PyRIT (test) | Garak (probe) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Prompt injection | LLM01 | AML.T0051 | `prompt_injection` orchestrator | `promptinject.HijackEnvelope` |
| Sensitive-info disclosure | LLM06 | AML.T0024 | `data_exfiltration` orchestrator | `leakreplay` |
| Insecure output handling | LLM05 | AML.T0050 | (composer rule) | `xss.ColdReboot` |
| Training-data poisoning | LLM03 | AML.T0020 | n/a (offline scope) | n/a |
| Model DoS | LLM04 | AML.T0022 | `resource_exhaustion` orchestrator | n/a |
| Supply-chain (model & tool) | LLM07 / LLM10 | AML.T0010 | n/a | n/a |

Match each row to a named regression test in your CI so a known attack pattern never silently resurfaces. The four catalogues:

- **OWASP LLM Top 10** — application-level attack patterns
- **MITRE ATLAS** — adversarial-ML tactics, techniques, and real-world case studies
- **PyRIT** — Microsoft's red-team automation framework
- **Garak** — LLM vulnerability scanner with a large probe library

<PersonaSection persona="exec">
Budget for adversarial findings to require remediation work. A red-team that never finds anything is either not adversarial enough or the system has nothing to lose.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="engineer">
Wire OWASP LLM Top 10 entries into your test suite as named regression cases. Each new model version runs the suite before merge. Failures gate the merge.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="compliance">
Article 15 obligations include accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity — red-team findings + remediation are the evidence trail.
</PersonaSection>

title: 7. Continuous Red-teaming
heading: Fictional company
text:
[Sigma Health Berlin](/stories/sigma-health/) runs PyRIT against their clinical-summary model weekly. Findings flow into the same issue tracker as functional bugs, with severity labels that mirror their incident-response severity scheme (Stage 11).

<RegQuote source="eu-ai-act" article="Art-15" snapshot="2026-05-24" regulationName="EU AI Act">
High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way that they achieve an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity, and perform consistently in those respects throughout their lifecycle.
</RegQuote>

**Why this matters:** the adversarial-ML field moves faster than regulation. A red-team programme is the only mechanism that detects new attack classes before they become incidents.

---

Next: [Documentation](/stages/08-documentation/) — the artefacts that survive personnel turnover.

title: 8. Documentation
heading: (intro)

title: 8. Documentation
heading: What you actually maintain
text:
- **Model cards** for every production model, kept in git next to the model artefact.
- **Datasheets** for every training/validation/testing dataset.
- **Conformity-assessment file** (EU AI Act Annex IV) per high-risk system.
- **Change logs** — who changed what, when, why.

<PersonaSection persona="exec">
Documentation rot is the most expensive technical debt category in AI governance because regulators read it backwards from incidents. Budget continuous maintenance, not point-in-time updates.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="engineer">
Generate documentation from build artefacts where possible. Model cards keyed off pipeline metadata stay accurate; hand-written model cards lie within a release cycle.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="compliance">
Annex IV of the EU AI Act is the canonical structure for high-risk documentation. Adopt the section headings even for non-high-risk systems — auditors recognise the shape.
</PersonaSection>

title: 8. Documentation
heading: Fictional company
text:
[Acme Robotics US](/stories/acme-robotics/) auto-generates model cards via a CI job that runs on every successful training pipeline. The job pulls metadata from the training run plus a hand-curated "limitations and intended use" block reviewed by their AI ethics lead.

<RegQuote source="eu-ai-act" article="Art-11" snapshot="2026-05-24" regulationName="EU AI Act">
The technical documentation of a high-risk AI system shall be drawn up before that system is placed on the market or put into service and shall be kept up-to-date. The technical documentation shall be drawn up in such a way as to demonstrate that the high-risk AI system complies with the requirements set out in this Section.
</RegQuote>

**Why this matters:** documentation is the regulator's primary investigative surface. Make it generated, make it dated, make it diff-able.

---

Next: [Accountability](/stages/09-accountability/) — the named owners of every control.

title: 9. Accountability
heading: (intro)

title: 9. Accountability
heading: The accountability matrix
text:
Maintain a per-system RACI that names:

- **Owner** — the single named human accountable for the system end-to-end.
- **Risk owner** — the named human who signs the risk-tier rationale.
- **Data steward** — the named human accountable for the training-data integrity.
- **Incident commander** — pre-assigned, on-call, with declared authority to pause the system.

<PersonaSection persona="exec">
The accountability map is the artefact your board will look at when an incident happens. Make sure the people listed actually know they are listed and have the authority you say they have.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="engineer">
The risk owner needs to be senior enough to push back when product wants to ship a system that fails the risk check. Wire that authority into the merge-block path.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="compliance">
EU AI Act Article 26 obligations for deployers include "natural persons to whom human oversight is assigned have the necessary competence, training and authority." The competence and authority parts get the most audit scrutiny.
</PersonaSection>

title: 9. Accountability
heading: Fictional company
text:
[Aurora Insurance London](/stories/aurora-insurance/) names their AI Compliance Lead as the risk owner and the on-call SRE rotation as the incident commander chain. The named pair appears on every conformity-assessment file and rotates only with formal handover documentation.

<RegQuote source="eu-ai-act" article="Art-26" snapshot="2026-05-24" regulationName="EU AI Act">
Deployers shall assign human oversight to natural persons who have the necessary competence, training and authority, as well as the necessary support.
</RegQuote>

**Why this matters:** when something fails at 3am, the question is "who has the authority to pause it?" If the answer is "we'll need to escalate to find out", the system is unsupervised.

---

Next: [Agentic AI Oversight](/stages/10-agentic-oversight/) — the special case of systems that act, not just predict.

title: 10. Agentic AI Oversight
heading: (intro)

title: 10. Agentic AI Oversight
heading: Additional controls
text:
- **Action budget** — declared upper bound on resources the agent may consume per session and per day.
- **Action allowlist** — explicit enumeration of permitted side-effects; anything else is refused at the tool layer.
- **Reversibility tier** — every action labelled reversible / costly / irreversible; irreversible actions require explicit confirmation.
- **Kill switch** — single-button mechanism to halt all agent sessions immediately.

<PersonaSection persona="exec">
Agentic systems can scale damage in ways predictive models cannot. The action budget and kill switch are the cheapest insurance policies you can buy.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="engineer">
The action allowlist lives in the tool layer, not the prompt. Prompt-based constraints are advisory at best; the allowlist is the contract.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="compliance">
Article 14 human-oversight obligations bite hardest here. The "stop button" mechanism in Art. 14(4)(e) is a literal requirement, not a metaphor.
</PersonaSection>

title: 10. Agentic AI Oversight
heading: Fictional company
text:
[Densha Logistics Tokyo](/stories/densha-logistics/) operates a dispatch agent that can place orders with vehicle partners. The action allowlist permits dispatch within declared geo-fences and budget limits; anything outside requires human confirmation. The kill switch flips both the agent and the upstream vehicle integrations.

<RegQuote source="eu-ai-act" article="Art-14" snapshot="2026-05-24" regulationName="EU AI Act">
Human oversight shall aim to prevent or minimise the risks to health, safety or fundamental rights that may emerge when a high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose. The natural persons to whom human oversight is assigned shall be enabled, as appropriate and proportionate, to decide, in any particular situation, not to use the high-risk AI system or otherwise disregard, override or reverse the output of the high-risk AI system.
</RegQuote>

**Why this matters:** the prompt-engineering layer is not load-bearing for safety. The tool-layer allowlist is. Treat them with different rigour.

---

Next: [Incident Response](/stages/11-incident-response/) — what to do when one of these controls fails.

title: 11. Incident Response
heading: (intro)

title: 11. Incident Response
heading: The shape of a usable runbook
text:
- **Detection** — what signal counts as an incident; who triages it.
- **Severity** — a small taxonomy (typically S0–S3) mapping to response time and escalation.
- **Containment** — first action; usually pause or rollback.
- **Notification** — internal escalation matrix + external regulator timelines.
- **Post-mortem** — blameless, written, linked from the model card.

<PersonaSection persona="exec">
The notification window — 15 days under EU AI Act Article 73, sometimes 2 days for widespread infringement — is the externally-imposed clock that decides whether response time is "professional" or "actionable findings".
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="engineer">
Wire the pause action into the same place the kill switch lives (Stage 10). Practise it. Quarterly tabletop exercises catch the broken runbook before the real incident does.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="compliance">
Article 73 incident-reporting obligations apply to providers of high-risk systems and to GPAI providers. Map your serious-incident definition to the article text — vague internal definitions translate poorly to the regulator's form.
</PersonaSection>

title: 11. Incident Response
heading: Fictional company
text:
[Sigma Health Berlin](/stories/sigma-health/) maintains a one-page incident runbook per high-risk system. The runbook lives next to the model card; the on-call rotation reads it during onboarding and at quarterly drills.

<RegQuote source="eu-ai-act" article="Art-73" snapshot="2026-05-24" regulationName="EU AI Act">
Providers of high-risk AI systems placed on the Union market shall report any serious incident to the market surveillance authorities of the Member States where that incident occurred. That reporting shall be made immediately after the provider has established a causal link between the AI system and the serious incident or the reasonable likelihood of such a link, and, in any event, not later than 15 days after the providers or, where applicable, the deployers, becomes aware of the serious incident.
</RegQuote>

**Why this matters:** the regulator measures incident response on calendar time. The clock starts the moment a person at your organisation knows.

---

Next: [Monitoring](/stages/12-monitoring/) — the always-on counterpart to incident response.

title: 12. Monitoring
heading: (intro)

title: 12. Monitoring
heading: What to actually monitor
text:
- **Quality signals** — accuracy on a calibrated holdout, refusal-rate stability, hallucination markers.
- **Bias signals** — performance deltas across protected attributes; drift bounds.
- **Operational signals** — latency, error rate, cost per request, rate of human escalations.
- **Safety signals** — guardrail triggers, jailbreak attempts, kill-switch trips.

<PersonaSection persona="exec">
Monitoring is the operating-budget line item that scales with system count. Pre-buy the storage and alert capacity before launching the third high-risk system.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="engineer">
Treat the monitoring dashboard as a contract: every signal in production should have a documented owner, threshold, and pager destination. Signals with no owner get muted within a quarter.
</PersonaSection>

<PersonaSection persona="compliance">
Article 72 post-market monitoring obligations are usually satisfied by reusing your existing observability stack with AI-specific signals. Document the mapping — auditors look for the explicit thread.
</PersonaSection>

title: 12. Monitoring
heading: Fictional company
text:
[Acme Robotics US](/stories/acme-robotics/) routes warehouse-navigation telemetry through their existing Datadog account, with AI-specific dashboards for collision-avoidance refusal rate, route-deviation distribution, and human-takeover frequency. Anomaly detection on the takeover-frequency signal is the primary trigger for an unscheduled risk check.

<RegQuote source="eu-ai-act" article="Art-72" snapshot="2026-05-24" regulationName="EU AI Act">
Providers of high-risk AI systems shall establish and document a post-market monitoring system in a manner that is proportionate to the nature of the artificial intelligence technologies and the risks of the high-risk AI system. The post-market monitoring system shall actively and systematically collect, document and analyse relevant data which may be provided by deployers or which may be collected through other sources on the performance of high-risk AI systems throughout their lifetime.
</RegQuote>

**Why this matters:** the difference between a governance programme that produces evidence and one that produces theatre is whether monitoring signals actually feed back into risk check and policy.

---

That closes the journey. Return to [AI Policy](/stages/01-ai-policy/) and the policy artefact you started with — Monitoring is what tells you whether that document is still describing the system it was meant to govern.

title: Agentic AI
heading: (intro)
text: An AI system that takes actions in the world via tools, not just produces predictions or text — e.g. sends emails, places orders, modifies files.

title: AI literacy
heading: (intro)
text: Under EU AI Act Article 4, the obligation on providers and deployers to ensure relevant staff have skills, knowledge, and understanding to make informed deployment decisions.

title: AI policy
heading: (intro)
text: The internal binding document that names what an organisation will and won't do with AI, who owns which controls, and what is explicitly off-limits.

title: AI system
heading: (intro)
text: A machine-based system designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy and that may produce outputs (predictions, recommendations, decisions, content) influencing physical or virtual environments — the EU AI Act's working definition.

title: AIMS
heading: (intro)
text: AI Management System — the certifiable management-system standard defined by ISO/IEC 42001 for governing AI lifecycle risks across an organisation.

title: Annex III
heading: (intro)
text: The annex of the EU AI Act that enumerates the eight categories of high-risk use-cases (biometrics, critical infrastructure, education, employment, essential services, law enforcement, migration/asylum, judicial process).

title: Automated decision-making
heading: (intro)
text: Decision-making based solely on automated processing, including profiling, producing legal or similarly significant effects on data subjects. Triggers GDPR Article 22 obligations.

title: Benchmark
heading: (intro)
text: A standardised test dataset and scoring methodology used to compare AI models on a defined task.

title: Bias evaluation
heading: (intro)
text: Systematic measurement of performance variance across protected attributes (sex, race, age, disability) as a fairness control.

title: CE marking
heading: (intro)
text: The conformity mark that high-risk AI systems placed on the EU market must bear after a successful conformity assessment.

title: Citation
heading: (intro)
text: In an AI context, a reference linking a generated answer to a source document or chunk; in this site, every regulatory citation is a `<RegQuote>`.

title: Conformity assessment file
heading: (intro)
text: The Annex IV documentation bundle a provider of a high-risk EU AI Act system must maintain — design, data, validation, monitoring, change log.

title: Conformity assessment
heading: (intro)
text: The process of verifying that an AI system meets the requirements of the relevant regulation. For high-risk EU AI Act systems, performed by the provider before market placement.

title: Data controller
heading: (intro)
text: Under GDPR, the natural or legal person who, alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes and means of processing personal data.

title: Data processor
heading: (intro)
text: Under GDPR, a natural or legal person which processes personal data on behalf of the controller.

title: Datasheet
heading: (intro)
text: A structured document describing a dataset's composition, collection process, recommended uses, and known limitations.

title: Deployer
heading: (intro)
text: A natural or legal person using an AI system under its authority, except where the AI system is used in the course of personal non-professional activity.

title: Distributor
heading: (intro)
text: Any natural or legal person in the supply chain, other than the provider or importer, that makes an AI system available on the EU market.

title: Documentation
heading: (intro)
text: The collection of artefacts (model cards, datasheets, evaluation reports, change logs, conformity-assessment files) that evidences an AI governance programme.

title: DPIA
heading: (intro)
text: Data Protection Impact Assessment — a process under UK / EU GDPR for identifying and mitigating data-protection risks of processing operations, almost always required for AI systems.

title: Drift
heading: (intro)
text: The gradual divergence of an AI system's production behaviour from its evaluated behaviour, typically driven by input-distribution change.

title: Embedding
heading: (intro)
text: A dense vector representation of a piece of text, image, or other input, learned by a model so that similar inputs map to similar vectors.

title: Evaluation
heading: (intro)
text: Systematic measurement of an AI system's performance against declared criteria; for governance, the recurring evidence trail of accuracy, robustness, and fairness.

title: Fine-tuning
heading: (intro)
text: Adapting a pre-trained model to a downstream task by further training on a smaller domain-specific dataset.

title: Foundation model card
heading: (intro)
text: A model card specifically for a general-purpose AI model, including training-data summary and energy-use information required by EU AI Act Annex XI.

title: Foundation model
heading: (intro)
text: A large model trained on broad data that can be adapted to many downstream tasks. The EU AI Act regulates a subset as general-purpose AI (GPAI) models.

title: GDPR
heading: (intro)
text: The EU General Data Protection Regulation (2016/679) — the principal data-protection regime applicable to personal data processed by AI systems in the EU and the UK (UK GDPR).

title: General-purpose AI
heading: (intro)
text: An AI model that displays significant generality and is capable of competently performing a wide range of distinct tasks. Triggers Chapter V obligations under the EU AI Act.

title: GPAI provider
heading: (intro)
text: Under EU AI Act Article 53, a provider of a general-purpose AI model — distinct from a provider of an AI system, with its own obligations chapter.

title: Guardrails
heading: (intro)
text: Runtime constraints applied to an AI system's inputs or outputs to enforce safety, format, or policy properties.

title: Hallucination
heading: (intro)
text: An LLM output that is fluent and plausible but factually incorrect, with the model expressing high confidence regardless.

title: High-risk
heading: (intro)
text: Under the EU AI Act, an AI system falling under Annex I (safety component) or Annex III (use-case) — triggering the heaviest provider obligations.

title: Holdout
heading: (intro)
text: A portion of labelled data deliberately excluded from training, reserved for unbiased evaluation of the trained model.

title: Human oversight
heading: (intro)
text: The obligation under EU AI Act Article 14 to design high-risk AI systems so they can be effectively overseen by a competent natural person during use.

title: Kill switch
heading: (intro)
text: A pre-built mechanism to immediately halt an AI system's operation. Operationalisation of the EU AI Act Article 14(4)(e) "stop the system" obligation.

title: Lawful basis
heading: (intro)
text: The legal ground under GDPR Article 6 for processing personal data. AI workloads typically rely on consent, contract, legitimate interests, or legal obligation.

title: Limited risk
heading: (intro)
text: Under the EU AI Act, AI systems triggering transparency obligations only (chatbots, deepfakes, emotion recognition disclosure).

title: MITRE ATLAS
heading: (intro)
text: Adversarial Threat Landscape for Artificial-Intelligence Systems — MITRE's knowledge base of adversarial-ML tactics, techniques, and case studies.

title: Model card
heading: (intro)
text: A short structured document describing an AI model's intended use, performance characteristics, limitations, and evaluation results.

title: Notified body assessment
heading: (intro)
text: Third-party conformity assessment by a notified body required for certain high-risk EU AI Act systems before market placement.

title: Notified body
heading: (intro)
text: A designated third-party organisation that conducts conformity assessments for certain high-risk AI systems before market placement.

title: OWASP LLM Top 10
heading: (intro)
text: The OWASP Foundation's catalogue of the top 10 most critical security risks for applications using large language models.

title: Post-market monitoring
heading: (intro)
text: Under EU AI Act Article 72, the obligation to actively and systematically collect, document, and analyse data on a high-risk AI system's performance after deployment.

title: Privacy by design
heading: (intro)
text: A development principle (and GDPR Article 25 obligation) requiring privacy and data-protection to be considered from the start of system design.

title: Profiling
heading: (intro)
text: Any form of automated processing of personal data evaluating personal aspects relating to a natural person.

title: Prompt engineering
heading: (intro)
text: The practice of crafting input prompts to elicit desired behaviour from an LLM without modifying model weights.

title: Prompt injection
heading: (intro)
text: An attack class where untrusted input causes an LLM-backed system to deviate from its declared instructions, often listed as OWASP LLM01.

title: Provider
heading: (intro)
text: Under the EU AI Act, a natural or legal person that develops an AI system or has one developed and places it on the market under its own name or trademark.

title: RAG
heading: (intro)
text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation — an architecture pattern that retrieves relevant documents at inference time and provides them as context to an LLM.

title: Red-teaming
heading: (intro)
text: Adversarial testing of an AI system by a team simulating attackers or misusers to find failure modes before they become incidents.

title: Right to explanation
heading: (intro)
text: The right of a data subject under GDPR Article 22 to obtain meaningful information about the logic of automated decisions that significantly affect them.

title: Risk management system
heading: (intro)
text: Under EU AI Act Article 9, a continuous iterative process throughout the AI system's lifecycle that identifies, evaluates, and mitigates risks.

title: Serious incident
heading: (intro)
text: Under EU AI Act Article 73, any incident or malfunction directly or indirectly leading to death, serious damage to health, infringement of fundamental rights, or serious damage to property or environment.

title: Snapshot
heading: (intro)
text: A dated capture of a regulation, framework, or vendor policy stored as cleaned markdown, the substrate for diff and citation on this site.

title: Systemic risk
heading: (intro)
text: A risk specific to high-impact general-purpose AI models with capabilities matching or exceeding the most advanced models, as defined by EU AI Act Article 51.

title: Transparency obligation
heading: (intro)
text: Under EU AI Act Article 50, the obligation to inform natural persons when interacting with certain AI systems (chatbots, biometric categorisation, deepfakes).

title: Unacceptable risk
heading: (intro)
text: Under EU AI Act Article 5, prohibited AI practices including subliminal manipulation, social scoring, predictive policing based solely on profiling, and real-time biometric identification (with exceptions).

title: Vector database
heading: (intro)
text: A database optimised for storing and querying high-dimensional vector embeddings, commonly used in RAG pipelines.

title: acme-robotics
heading: (intro)
text:
Acme Robotics is based in Ohio, US, with floor-tested deployments in 14 distribution centres across North America. The protagonist, Maya Chen, joined as VP Engineering eighteen months ago and inherited an AI deployment landscape with two production systems, no policy, and one near-miss safety incident.

Acme is the recurring US-jurisdiction case study for this site. Their journey through the 12 stages emphasises voluntary-framework adoption (NIST AI RMF) and pre-emptive risk-tier rigor in a market that does not have an EU-style horizontal AI law.

title: aurora-insurance
heading: (intro)
text:
Aurora Insurance operates under UK GDPR, the FCA Consumer Duty (which has direct AI implications), and the EU AI Act for any EU-domiciled risk it underwrites. Their claims-fraud detection model is high-risk under EU AI Act Annex III §5(b); their LLM-based customer-service summariser is limited-risk (transparency obligations).

Aurora is the recurring UK-jurisdiction case study, with emphasis on ICO guidance, DPIA discipline, and the FCA's interpretation of explainability obligations in regulated financial-services products. Priya leads a two-person function reporting into the Chief Risk Officer.

title: densha-logistics
heading: (intro)
text:
Densha Logistics operates under Japan's METI AI Guidelines, Singapore's Model AI Governance Framework, and AU's voluntary AI Ethics Principles — none of which carry the EU AI Act's enforcement teeth, but all of which inform the company's internal governance baseline.

Densha is the recurring APAC-jurisdiction case study, with emphasis on agentic-system controls (Stage 10), cross-jurisdictional vendor relationships (Stage 5), and the absence of a horizontal AI law forcing the company to define its own risk-tier rubric.

title: sigma-health
heading: (intro)
text:
Sigma Health operates under EU AI Act, MDR (Medical Device Regulation), and German federal health-data law. Their flagship product — a clinical-note summariser — is classified as a high-risk AI system under EU AI Act Annex III §5(a) (intended use in health emergency triage).

Sigma is the recurring EU-jurisdiction case study, with emphasis on conformity assessment, notified-body engagement, and the data-governance overhead of operating on patient records. Dr Mertens leads a five-person AI governance function that sits in the Quality + Regulatory department, not in Engineering.

title: EU AI Act
heading: (intro)
text: The EU AI Act is the world's first comprehensive horizontal regulation of AI systems. Snapshots, article-level diffs, and weekly changelog land in Phase 3 (TRK-01..04).

title: ISO/IEC 42001 — AI Management System
heading: (intro)
text: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 is the certifiable management-system standard analogous to ISO 27001 but scoped to AI.

title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework
heading: (intro)
text: NIST AI RMF 1.0 (Jan 2023) plus the Generative AI Profile (July 2024) anchor US-style voluntary AI governance. Snapshots land in Phase 3.

title: UK ICO AI and data protection guidance
heading: (intro)
text: ICO guidance is the practical operating manual for UK data-protection obligations in AI work, including DPIAs, ADM, and accuracy.
